ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a gyoza-specific term; 'dumpling food product' (FOODON_03302050) is the closest defensible parent class. Gyoza is a pan-fried Japanese dumpling variant.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers filled dumplings/pasta broadly; no gyoza-specific code exists. Used as closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for gyoza or Japanese dumplings as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of gyoza is 6 pieces, weighing approximately 120 g, commonly served as a starter or side dish in Japanese restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Gyoza (Fried Dumpling) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F06100016 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status 'reviewed'. All per_100g values are taken from this canonical HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is set to -1. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, cholesterol, vitamins, and water are not reported by HPB for this entry and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for gyoza exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated by analogy to wheat-flour-wrapped filled dumplings (wonton/dumpling category, GI ~55, medium) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The wheat flour wrapper is the primary glycaemic driver; pork filling and dietary fibre (2.0 g/100g) moderate the glycaemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 55 × 22.2 / 100 ≈ 12.2 (based on 120 g serving providing 22.2 g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 375 mg/100g with minimal potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Gyoza contains 375 mg sodium per 100 g (450 mg per standard 120 g serving), approaching 20% of the WHO daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portions to 3–4 pieces and avoid high-sodium soy-based dipping sauces to reduce total sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (18.5 g/100g) with dietary fibre and protein moderating glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Gyoza has an estimated GI of 55 (low) and provides 18.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g; the 2.0 g dietary fibre and 8.2 g protein slow gastric emptying and moderate postprandial glucose rise, making a portion of 4–5 pieces (80–100 g) manageable within an ADA-aligned meal plan provided high-sugar dipping sauces are avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and pan-frying fat contribution with unquantified saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The sodium content (375 mg/100g) and pan-frying process increase cardiovascular risk; saturated fat data are unavailable from the HPB source but pork-filled pan-fried dumplings typically contribute 2–3 g saturated fat per 100 g, and individuals with CVD should limit portions to 3–4 pieces, choose steamed variants where available, and avoid salty dipping sauces.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Arachidonic acid from pork and advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) from pan-frying
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ground pork is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies pro-inflammatory eicosanoid pathways relevant to RA; pan-frying generates AGEs that worsen joint inflammation, and individuals with RA should prefer steamed gyoza with chicken or tofu filling as a lower-inflammatory alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Gyoza (餃子; gyōza) are Japanese pan-fried dumplings with a thin wheat-flour wrapper encasing a seasoned filling of minced pork, chives (nira), garlic, ginger, soy sauce, and sesame oil. The cooking technique — steaming then pan-frying — produces a crispy golden base with a tender, juicy top, giving gyoza their characteristic dual texture. Originating from Chinese jiaozi, gyoza were adapted in Japan during the 20th century and are now a staple of Japanese cuisine served in ramen restaurants, izakayas, and as a popular street food. Per 100 g, gyoza provides 179 kcal, 8.2 g protein, 8.0 g fat, 18.5 g carbohydrate, 2.0 g dietary fibre, and 375 mg sodium. The moderate carbohydrate content and dietary fibre give gyoza an estimated low glycemic index of approximately 55. Clinically, the sodium content is the primary concern for individuals with hypertension and cardiovascular disease, while the moderate carbohydrate load requires portion awareness for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The pork filling and pan-frying method are relevant considerations for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to arachidonic acid and AGE content. Gyoza are commonly served with a dipping sauce of soy sauce, rice vinegar, and chilli oil, which can significantly increase the sodium load of the meal.
common_user_queries: What is the difference between gyoza and jiaozi?
Can I eat gyoza with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06100016, review_status 'reviewed', confidence 0.95), which is an approved primary regional source. GI is estimated by analogy from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest defensible wheat-flour dumpling category, which is documented. Ontology codes are approximate but defensible.
notes: 1. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, cholesterol, all vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water_g are set to -1: these fields are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for F06100016 (Gyoza) as of April 2026. Detailed fatty acid profile and micronutrient data for this specific preparation are not available in the HPB database or USDA FoodData Central for an exact gyoza match. 2. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on wheat-flour-wrapped filled dumplings from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for gyoza exists in approved sources. 3. Glycemic load per serving calculated as 55 × 22.2 g carbohydrate (120 g serving) / 100 = 12.2. 4. Halal status is not confirmed; traditional gyoza contains pork and is not halal. 5. FoodOn URI FOODON_03302050 and FoodEx2 code A0FKP are approximate matches; no gyoza-specific ontology codes exist in these systems as of 2026.
